icd_category: D35.0 — Benign neoplasm of adrenal gland
one_liner: A 38-year-old female undergoing laparoscopic adrenalectomy for a suspected adrenal incidentaloma develops sudden severe hypertensive crisis (BP 260/140), tachycardia, and ventricular arrhythmias during tumor manipulation.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient is a 38-year-old female who was incidentally found to have a 4.2 cm right adrenal mass on CT performed 2 months ago for evaluation of recurrent kidney stones. She was referred to surgery for evaluation. In retrospect, when specifically asked, she endorsed episodic headaches (2-3 per month, severe, throbbing), palpitations, and diaphoretic episodes lasting 15-30 minutes that she attributed to anxiety and perimenopause. She has had difficulty controlling her blood pressure despite two antihypertensive medications. She denied flushing, weight loss, abdominal pain, or visual changes. The biochemical workup was ordered by her primary care physician but results were not yet available when the surgery was scheduled. The surgeon proceeded with laparoscopic right adrenalectomy for what was presumed to be a nonfunctioning adrenal adenoma based on imaging characteristics. During the operation, upon insufflation and initial manipulation of the adrenal gland, the patient suddenly developed severe hypertension (BP 260/140 mmHg), heart rate 148 bpm, and ventricular tachycardia on the monitor. The surgery was halted. This is the presentation at the time of the intraoperative crisis.
stage2.pmh: Resistant hypertension (on 2 medications, poorly controlled), recurrent nephrolithiasis, episodic headaches, anxiety disorder (diagnosed 2 years ago)
stage2.medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily, lisinopril 40 mg daily, alprazolam 0.5 mg as needed
stage2.family_history: Father with hypertension and thyroid nodule. Paternal uncle died suddenly at age 42 of 'cardiac arrest'. No known family history of endocrine neoplasia syndromes.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. Rare alcohol. Works as an architect. Married with 1 child. No illicit drugs. Exercises regularly (notes palpitations during exercise).
stage2.physical_exam: Patient is under general anesthesia, intubated, with laparoscopic ports in place. The surgical field shows a right adrenal mass with prominent vascularity. The tumor appears brownish-yellow with a distinct capsule. Skin is notably diaphoretic and flushed. Monitor shows wide-complex tachycardia (ventricular tachycardia). Arterial line shows systolic pressures of 250-270 mmHg with beat-to-beat variation. Central venous catheter in place. Abdomen is insufflated with CO2. The anesthesiologist reports that the blood pressure and heart rate spiked immediately upon tumor manipulation and that all inhalational and IV anesthetic agents are at adequate levels, ruling out light anesthesia.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated (>4x upper limit), diagnostic of catecholamine-secreting tumor. These results were pending at the time of surgery, representing a critical systems failure.

lab_results.result: Norepinephrine 486 mcg/24hr (normal <80), Epinephrine 214 mcg/24hr (normal <20), Dopamine 312 mcg/24hr (normal <400)
lab_results.clinical_significance: Massively elevated urine catecholamines confirming pheochromocytoma

lab_results.result: pH 7.24, pCO2 32, pO2 88, HCO3 14, Lactate 6.2 mmol/L
lab_results.clinical_significance: Mixed metabolic acidosis with lactic acidosis from catecholamine-induced vasoconstriction and tissue hypoperfusion
Catecholamine-induced glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis; hyperglycemia is expected during pheochromocytoma crisis
Elevated, indicating myocardial injury from catecholamine excess (demand ischemia, catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy, or direct cardiotoxicity)
Hypokalemia from catecholamine-induced intracellular potassium shift; contributes to arrhythmia risk
Mildly elevated; should prompt later evaluation for MEN2 syndrome (hyperparathyroidism)
Elevated, consistent with chronic catecholamine-induced volume contraction (hemoconcentration)
Markedly elevated, consistent with neuroendocrine tumor (pheochromocytoma)

imaging_results.test_name: Pre-operative CT Abdomen with IV Contrast
imaging_results.result: A 4.2 x 3.8 cm well-circumscribed right adrenal mass. Heterogeneous enhancement pattern with areas of central necrosis. Pre-contrast density 38 HU (>10 HU). Absolute contrast washout of 42% at 15 minutes (<60%). No calcifications. No invasion of adjacent structures. Left adrenal normal. No lymphadenopathy.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: The pre-contrast density >10 HU and washout <60% are NOT consistent with a benign adenoma and should have raised suspicion for pheochromocytoma or adrenocortical carcinoma. A lipid-poor adenoma was incorrectly presumed.

imaging_results.result: Monomorphic ventricular tachycardia at rate 148 bpm. ST-segment depression in lateral leads. Following phentolamine administration, converted to sinus tachycardia at 118 bpm with residual ST depression.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Catecholamine-induced ventricular arrhythmia with myocardial ischemia. Responded to alpha-blockade, supporting catecholamine excess as the etiology.

key_tests: Plasma fractionated metanephrines (should have been obtained pre-operatively)
CT adrenal protocol (pre-contrast HU measurement, washout characteristics)
acceptable_tests: MIBG scan (for metastatic disease or extra-adrenal disease)
Genetic testing (RET, VHL, SDHB/C/D — for hereditary syndromes)
low_yield_tests: Vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) — older, less sensitive test replaced by metanephrines
Clonidine suppression test (rarely needed with markedly elevated metanephrines)
Adrenal biopsy (absolutely contraindicated without prior biochemical workup — can precipitate lethal crisis)
stage3.final_clue: Intraoperative hypertensive crisis with BP 260/140, ventricular tachycardia, and lactic acidosis triggered by adrenal tumor manipulation is classic for undiagnosed pheochromocytoma. Post-hoc plasma metanephrines are massively elevated. The adrenal mass had imaging features (HU >10, low washout) that were inconsistent with a benign adenoma. This case represents a failure to complete the mandatory biochemical workup before adrenalectomy — all adrenal incidentalomas require functional assessment before surgical intervention.

stage5.final_clue: Intraoperative hypertensive crisis with BP 260/140, ventricular tachycardia, and lactic acidosis triggered by adrenal tumor manipulation is classic for undiagnosed pheochromocytoma. Post-hoc plasma metanephrines are massively elevated. The adrenal mass had imaging features (HU >10, low washout) that were inconsistent with a benign adenoma. This case represents a failure to complete the mandatory biochemical workup before adrenalectomy — all adrenal incidentalomas require functional assessment before surgical intervention.

debrief.classic_presentation: The classic triad of pheochromocytoma is episodic headache, diaphoresis, and palpitations, occurring in paroxysms. Sustained or paroxysmal hypertension is present in >90% of patients. The 'Rule of 10s' (historically): 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% malignant, 10% familial, 10% in children — though modern data show higher rates of hereditary disease (up to 40%). Intraoperative crisis occurs when an undiagnosed pheochromocytoma is manipulated during surgery, triggering massive catecholamine release. This is a preventable catastrophe that underscores the importance of biochemical screening for all adrenal incidentalomas.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: This case is a near-miss scenario that highlights critical systems issues and intraoperative crisis management: (1) Every adrenal incidentaloma must undergo biochemical evaluation BEFORE surgery: plasma fractionated metanephrines (sensitivity >96%), 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (exclude Cushing), and aldosterone/renin ratio if hypertensive. Operating on an unscreened adrenal mass is a never-event that can be lethal. (2) CT characteristics that should have raised suspicion: Hounsfield units >10 on non-contrast CT suggests a lipid-poor lesion (adenomas are typically <10 HU); absolute washout <60% further argues against adenoma. These features warrant more aggressive biochemical workup and possibly MRI or MIBG scan. (3) Intraoperative crisis management protocol: STOP tumor manipulation immediately. Notify the surgeon to halt the operation. IV phentolamine 2-5 mg boluses (alpha-blocker) for hypertension. IV esmolol or lidocaine for tachyarrhythmias — do NOT give beta-blockers before alpha-blockade (unopposed alpha stimulation causes paradoxical worsening of hypertension). IV magnesium sulfate 2g for refractory arrhythmias. IV nitroprusside for refractory hypertension. Volume resuscitate aggressively once alpha-blockade is established (catecholamine-induced vasoconstriction masks severe volume depletion). (4) Decision to continue vs. abort surgery: If the patient stabilizes with pharmacologic management and the surgeon can safely complete the resection, proceeding may be reasonable to avoid a second operation. If the patient remains unstable, abort the surgery, close, and plan for proper preoperative alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin for 10-14 days) before reattempting. (5) Post-resection hypotension: After tumor removal, expect precipitous hypotension from loss of catecholamine drive in a chronically volume-depleted patient — have vasopressors (phenylephrine, norepinephrine) and fluids immediately available. Also monitor for rebound hypoglycemia (loss of catecholamine-driven gluconeogenesis). (6) The mildly elevated calcium should prompt evaluation for MEN2A (pheochromocytoma + medullary thyroid carcinoma + hyperparathyroidism); genetic testing for RET proto-oncogene mutations is indicated.
debrief.pathophysiology: Pheochromocytomas are catecholamine-secreting tumors arising from chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla. They produce, store, and secrete catecholamines (norepinephrine, epinephrine, and rarely dopamine). During tumor manipulation, massive catecholamine release from the tumor into the systemic circulation causes: (1) Severe hypertension from alpha-1 receptor-mediated vasoconstriction. (2) Tachycardia and arrhythmias from beta-1 receptor stimulation. (3) Coronary vasospasm and myocardial oxygen demand mismatch, leading to myocardial injury. (4) Peripheral vasoconstriction causing end-organ ischemia and lactic acidosis. (5) Hyperglycemia from glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis stimulated by catecholamines. Chronic catecholamine excess causes volume contraction (vasoconstriction redistributes blood volume and causes pressure natriuresis), which becomes unmasked as severe hypotension after tumor resection. Catecholamine cardiomyopathy (Takotsubo-like) can occur from direct myocardial toxicity.
debrief.treatment_overview: Intraoperative crisis: Halt tumor manipulation. IV phentolamine (alpha-blocker) 2-5 mg boluses for hypertension. Nitroprusside infusion for refractory hypertension. Beta-blocker (esmolol) ONLY AFTER alpha-blockade is established. Lidocaine or amiodarone for ventricular arrhythmias. Magnesium sulfate 2g IV for refractory arrhythmias. Aggressive volume resuscitation once alpha-blockade achieved. Proper preoperative preparation (if surgery is aborted and replanned): Phenoxybenzamine (irreversible alpha-blocker) 10-20 mg BID, titrated up over 10-14 days until orthostatic hypotension develops, then add beta-blocker (only after adequate alpha-blockade). High-sodium diet and liberal fluids to expand contracted blood volume. Metyrosine (catecholamine synthesis inhibitor) in refractory cases. Surgery (laparoscopic adrenalectomy) after adequate alpha-blockade. Post-operative monitoring: watch for hypotension (volume resuscitation, vasopressors) and hypoglycemia (D10 infusion). Long-term: annual metanephrines to screen for recurrence. Genetic counseling and testing (RET, VHL, SDHx).
high_yield_pearls: All adrenal incidentalomas must undergo biochemical evaluation for pheochromocytoma (plasma metanephrines), cortisol excess (1 mg DST), and aldosterone excess (if hypertensive) BEFORE surgery.
Never give a beta-blocker before establishing alpha-blockade in pheochromocytoma — unopposed alpha stimulation causes paradoxical hypertensive crisis.
Plasma fractionated metanephrines have >96% sensitivity and are the recommended first-line screening test for pheochromocytoma.
CT characteristics suggestive of pheochromocytoma: Hounsfield units >10 on non-contrast, heterogeneous enhancement, and absolute washout <60%.
Up to 40% of pheochromocytomas are hereditary (MEN2A/2B, von Hippel-Lindau, SDH mutations, NF1) — all patients should be offered genetic counseling.
After tumor resection, expect hypotension (loss of catecholamine drive + chronic volume depletion) and hypoglycemia (loss of catecholamine-driven gluconeogenesis).
sources: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Pheochromocytoma and Paraganglioma (J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2014;99:1915-1942)
Schwartz's Principles of Surgery, 11th Edition, Ch. 38
Kinney MA et al. Anesth Analg 2000;91:1118-1123
